chunk_summary: This section discusses long-term complications and nutrition adaptations in cystic fibrosis (CF), focusing on therapy-driven strategies to manage CF-related diabetes, bone disease, and liver disease, as per established guidelines.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of nutrition in managing long-term complications of cystic fibrosis, including diabetes, bone disease, and liver disease.
Identify therapy-driven nutritional adaptations for cystic fibrosis patients.
Review evidence-based guidelines for nutrition care in cystic fibrosis from ESPEN, ESPGHAN, and ECFS.
Explore the impact of chronic cardiorespiratory disease on nutritional requirements in CF patients.
key_facts: Cystic fibrosis-related diabetes (CFRD) is a common complication, requiring tailored nutritional strategies to manage blood glucose levels (Reference 2).
Bone disease in CF patients is addressed through adequate intake of calcium and vitamin D, as per clinical care guidelines (Reference 3).
Cystic fibrosis liver disease (CFLD) affects up to 30% of CF patients, necessitating specific dietary modifications to support liver function (Reference 4).
ESPEN–ESPGHAN–ECFS guidelines emphasize the importance of high-calorie, high-protein diets for CF patients to meet increased energy demands (Reference 1).
Nutritional interventions should be individualized based on the patient’s age, disease severity, and presence of complications.
reference_quote: ESPEN–ESPGHAN–ECFS guidelines recommend high-calorie, high-protein diets to meet the increased energy demands of cystic fibrosis patients.
document_key_topics: Bone disease and liver disease in CF
